totalTime.note: Total time in seconds (15 questions × 90s = 1350s max). Longer time reduces Cognitive efficiency; negative contribution.

totalCorrect.note: Number of correct answers out of 15 questions. Validated: accuracy on SAT-style verbal items → crystallized/verbal ability (Intellectual).

totalQuestions.note: Total number of questions presented (should always be 15). Not used for scoring - informational only.

totalPercentage.note: Percentage correct (0-100%). Redundant with totalCorrect but useful for quick assessment.

totalSkipped.note: Number of skipped items (max 15). Treated as reduced persistence/engagement. Negative contribution.

totalHints.note: Total hints used across all questions (15 questions × 3 hints each = max 45). Higher hint usage indicates lower independence and self-efficacy. Negative contribution.

avgConfidence.note: Average confidence self-rating on 0-5 scale. Maps to self-efficacy and test anxiety (inverse).

mentalDemand.note: Mental demand adapted for verbal comprehension. Perceived difficulty reflects both task challenge and participant ability.

emotionalState.note: Emotional state during test. Maps to psychological resilience and stress tolerance. High calmness indicates low Neuroticism.

riddleImpact.note: Impact of riddles on engagement vs cognitive fatigue. Positive impact indicates resilience and adaptive learning.

bpm.note: Resting heart rate indicates autonomic nervous system regulation and stress levels. Optimal range is 60-80 BPM.

stressLevel.note: Self-reported stress (1-10 scale). REVERSE-SCORED: higher stress indicates higher Neuroticism and lower performance resilience.

focusLevel.note: Self-rated focus (1-10 scale). Predicts task accuracy and completion efficiency. High focus correlates with Conscientiousness.

stressChange.note: Self-assessed stress change compared to pre-test. Feeling less stressed indicates emotional regulation and adaptive coping.

environmentFamiliarity.note: Familiarity with academic/test environment affects behavioral comfort and performance baseline.

careerReflection.note: Self-reflection on career relevance of verbal skills. Indicates metacognitive awareness and future orientation.

futureEnvironment.note: Resonance with future environment indicates career maturity and goal-directedness. Workplace orientation scored highest.

hintUsage.note: Self-reported hint usage frequency. Correlates with totalHints metric. Independence proxy.

riddleSkipping.note: Riddle completion persistence. Negative skipping indicates low Conscientiousness and possible avoidance behavior.

approachToSAT.note: Problem-solving approach and resilience under difficulty. Persistence indicates high Conscientiousness and low Neuroticism.

strengthAwareness.note: Self-awareness of verbal abilities. Metacognitive insight correlates with Openness and self-monitoring (Conscientiousness).

careerReflectionStrength.note: Perceived link between test performance and real-world verbal ability. Self-efficacy indicator.

settingFutureSelf.note: Ability to imagine future success. Indicates psychological readiness and goal-directedness (future orientation).

finalTotalTime.note: Total time (s). Longer → lower efficiency. NormMax = 2000s.

finalTotalScore.note: Arithmetic/mental maths score → Intellectual. NormMax = max possible score.

finalTotalQuestions.note: Persistence/load measure — more attempted Qs = higher Behavioral/Cognitive.

finalTotalPercentage.note: Accuracy across items. Intellectual + Cognitive balance.

bpm.note: Resting heart rate indicates autonomic nervous system regulation and stress levels.

careerReflectionStrength.note: Perceived link between test performance and real-world ability.

careerConnection.note: Perceived career relevance of mathematical skills.

MemoryRecallTest_test2.note: Working memory / short-term memory block. Metrics map to validated constructs (WAIS working memory, n-back/span tasks, Baddeley & Hitch model). Surveys map to NASA-TLX (mental demand, frustration) and metacognitive/coping constructs.

totalTime.note: Total completion time (seconds). Longer time → lower processing efficiency under memory load; negative contribution.

totalLevelsCompleted.note: Number of memory levels successfully completed. Validated: recall sequences → working memory capacity / short-term memory span.

totalLevels.note: Total levels attempted (should always equal max 8). Persistence and tolerance for cognitive load.

totalPercentage.note: Percentage of levels completed successfully (0-100%). Maps primarily to Cognitive accuracy with a Behavioral component.

difficulty.note: NASA-TLX style mental demand — subjective cognitive load during memory task.

longerSequences.note: Emotional response to increasing memory load. Motivation under pressure vs. overload (behavioral & emotional response).

quitOrContinue.note: Direct behavioral measure of persistence / task withdrawal under cognitive load.

bpm.note: Resting heart rate indicates autonomic nervous system regulation and stress levels. Optimal range is 60-80 BPM.

stressLevel.note: Self-reported stress (1-10 scale). REVERSE-SCORED: Maps to NASA-TLX Frustration / emotional load under memory pressure.

focusLevel.note: Self-rated focus (1-10 scale). Sustained attention under working memory load (Cognitive domain).

stressChange.note: Self-reported stress change relative to pre-test baseline. Indicates stress reactivity and emotional regulation.

mistakeHandling.note: Error-coping strategy during memory failures. Strong predictor of resilience and sustained performance under cognitive challenge.

memoryStrategy.note: Metacognitive strategy use. Validated predictor of working memory performance (Baddeley & Hitch; strategy-based gains).

strengthAwareness.note: Self-awareness of memory strengths. Metacognitive insight and self-monitoring ability.

careerReflectionStrength.note: Perceived career relevance of memory skills. Influences engagement and persistence in test behavior.

ReactionTimerTest2_2.note: RT task — maps to processing speed (Cognitive), inhibitory control (Behavioral), and stress reactivity (Psychological).

avgReactionTime.note: Mean RT (milliseconds). Shorter = better processing speed/attentional control. NormMin: 150ms (elite athlete level, physiological limit ~120-150ms). NormMax: 500ms (slow but within normal range; 600ms+ indicates severe impairment).

finalReactionTime.note: Reaction time in final trials (milliseconds) — vigilance decrement / fatigue marker. Higher normMax (600ms) accounts for fatigue effects in later trials.

totalPenalty.note: Total penalties for premature clicks (false starts). Higher penalties = poor inhibitory control, impulsivity. NormMax: 10 penalties (severe impulsivity); most users 0-3.

waitingForGreen.note: Impulsivity vs. patience during anticipatory period. Matches Go/NoGo inhibitory control research.

penaltyFeeling.note: Emotional response to errors — relates to error monitoring and resilience. Motivated response indicates low Neuroticism.

reactionSpeed.note: Self-perceived speed, cross-validates measured RT. Metacognitive accuracy indicator.

bpm.note: Resting heart rate indicates autonomic nervous system regulation and stress levels. Optimal range is 60-80 BPM.

stressLevel.note: NASA-TLX Frustration dimension — REVERSE-SCORED: higher stress → worse psychological functioning.

focusLevel.note: Subjective attentional control (RT stability and consistency).

stressChange.note: Stress reactivity across the session. Recovery indicates emotional regulation.

impulseControl.note: Direct self-report measure of inhibitory control.

visualImpact.note: External environment / sensory distraction impact. Alert response indicates low Neuroticism and high Openness.

reflexAwareness.note: Metacognitive awareness of reflex ability; ties to self-monitoring and accurate self-assessment.

careerReflectionStrength.note: Motivational alignment with real-world application and career relevance.

NavigationGrid2.note: Spatial navigation and grid-based problem solving.

overallTotalTime.note: Total completion time (seconds). Longer = slower cognitive processing and possible stress overload. NormMax set to 1800s (30 min) to accommodate untimed nature while normalizing outliers.

overallTotalPercentage.note: Percentage of correct paths. Validated indicator of visuospatial working memory and reasoning accuracy.

avgAttempts.note: Average attempts per completed level (totalAttempts / completedLevels). More attempts = trial-and-error strategy, lower planning ability. NormMax: 10 attempts avg (severe planning deficit); optimal is 1-2 attempts.

totalLevels.note: Total number of levels in the test (always 10). Informational only, not used for scoring.

totalLevelsCompleted.note: Number of levels successfully completed out of 10 total. Indicates persistence and spatial reasoning ability.

spatialDemand.note: NASA-TLX Mental Demand adapted for visuospatial load. Higher perceived difficulty indicates cognitive challenge.

bpm.note: Resting heart rate indicates autonomic nervous system regulation and stress levels.

strategyuse.note: Strategic approach reflects executive function and planning.

focusLevel.note: Self-rated focus predicts task accuracy and completion time.

stressLevel.note: Self-reported stress during navigation task. REVERSE-SCORED.

When working toward a difficult goal, wh.note: Intrinsic motivation (achievement satisfaction) → stronger Psychological resilience. Extrinsic (recognition/reward) → Behavioral persistence. Deadlines map lower to Psychological because they drive controlled rather than autonomous motivation.

A project will take months to complete..note: Validated by Grit Scale (Duckworth et al.): Endurance with long-term goals maps to Psychological resilience; short-term preference maps negatively to Behavioral persistence.

When you hit a roadblock in your work, w.note: Persistence in problem solving → Behavioral domain. Seeking help also has Cognitive problem-solving implications.

Which best describes your work style?.note: Consistency and steady effort align with Behavioral persistence. External drive lowers Psychological autonomy.

Imagine receiving no recognition for you.note: Resilience to lack of recognition (intrinsic satisfaction) maps strongly to Psychological stability. Recognition reliance → extrinsic, Behavioral.

What motivates you most in school, uni,.note: SDT intrinsic: mastery → Cognitive & Psychological. Extrinsic (praise/fear/team) → Behavioral persistence. Fear of failure is controlled motivation, weaker overall.

You’re under deadline pressure when a fr.note: Cognitive load theory: prioritization and inhibition (finishing primary task first) maps to Cognitive control. Balancing maps partially to Behavioral persistence.

You’re juggling several roles in a proje.note: Executive functioning (task switching vs sustained attention). Focusing fully reflects higher Cognitive efficiency.

You’re in a timed exam with many section.note: Time-management strategies: systematic approaches (easy first, or balanced allocation) are validated predictors of Cognitive planning ability.

At work, two urgent requests arrive at o.note: Prioritising by deadlines is strongest Cognitive control. Behavioral factor reflects adapting under ambiguity.

In a group project, everyone depends on.note: Systematic organisation reflects Cognitive executive control. Juggling tasks reflects Behavioral perseverance, even under stress.

You’re interrupted constantly while work.note: Resisting interruptions reflects Psychological resilience; handling immediately reflects Behavioral compliance but less Cognitive efficiency.

A teacher or boss criticizes your work h.note: Maps to emotion regulation strategies. Calm listening = reappraisal (positive). Immediate defense = suppression (lower score).

You’re excluded from a group activity yo.note: Affective reaction scale. Moving on = adaptive coping. Anger/indifference reflect disengagement.

You make a mistake that affects your tea.note: Problem-focused coping. Admission and repair = high adaptive coping; blame = externalisation.

You’re waiting in a very long queue. Wha.note: Patience vs irritability maps to emotional tolerance and frustration control (self-regulation).

Someone takes credit for your work. How.note: Conflict management style. Calm confrontation/private discussion reflect assertive coping.

You’re under a lot of stress. How do you.note: Problem-focused (breaking tasks), social support, vs avoidant coping (distraction, procrastination).

Which type of work do you prefer?.note: Holland RIASEC primary work preference. Each answer maps to one type.

Which work environment suits you best?.note: RIASEC environment preference (E, C, A, S).

What personal value matters most in your.note: Values aligned to RIASEC (C, A, S, E).

How do you usually approach new challeng.note: Challenge approach style (I, C, E, R).

When given freedom in work, what do you.note: Work freedom preference (A, C, I, S).

When working on a project, you prefer to.note: Work style preference (R, E, C, A). Added to ensure R is sufficiently represented.

In a team setting, what role naturally f.note: Team role preference (I, A, S, C). Ensures all 6 types well-represented.

TIPIInventory.note: Ten-Item Personality Inventory (TIPI). 7-point Likert scale (1=Disagree strongly, 7=Agree strongly). Items 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 are reverse-scored.

motivationComparison.note: Motivational gain compared to paper-based testing.

realWorldFormat.note: Ecological validity measure — alignment with real-world stress and performance contexts.

patternAwareness.note: Metacognitive awareness of patterns across tasks.

leastTestedTrait.note: Exploratory — asks users which career traits were underrepresented.

mostTestedTrait.note: Self-perception of which domains were captured.

preferredFeature.note: User feedback dimension — exploratory, informs product design.

preferredAssessment.note: User format preference — anchors ecological validity and acceptance.
